_meta.issue: #171 Rebuild ścieżek — P2 Bezpieczne używanie AI
_meta.interaction: Klasyfikacja: zgodne z polityką vs shadow AI (kind: classify)
_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE przykłady SYNTETYCZNE (NotkaApp/SklepDemo, przyklad.test, Jan Syntetyczny). ZERO realnych danych/PII.

intro: Companies do not ban AI on principle — they organize WHAT you may work with and WITH WHICH data. This module explains what shadow AI is and why it does harm, how to read a company policy, what copyright means for generated content, and who is responsible for work produced with AI.

screens.title: Shadow AI: working in the dark

blocks.text: Using AI tools for work tasks without the company’s knowledge and consent — most often through private accounts in public chats.

The problem is not the tool itself but the lack of control: nobody knows what company and customer data is leaking out, under what terms it is processed, or whether it ends up training someone else’s model. Good work results do not offset this risk — it grows with every paste.

blocks.title: The quiet cost
blocks.text: A leak through shadow AI usually comes to light late and by accident — and by then it covers months of data, not a single message.

screens.title: Company policy: which tools and which data

blocks.text: A good AI policy answers two questions: which tools are approved and which categories of data may be processed in them. It is a map, not a wall — it shows the safe route instead of banning everything.

rows: General knowledge, drafts with no company data
only if the policy explicitly permits it
blocks.textAlt: Matrix: the more sensitive the data, the narrower the choice of tools — customer data only in tools explicitly permitted by the policy.

blocks.title: Found a new tool?
blocks.text: Found something better? Submit it for review (IT/security) instead of using it quietly. The approval path exists so that innovation does not come at the expense of security.

screens.title: Licenses and copyright for AI content

blocks.text: Content generated by AI is not “nobody’s” in any practical sense: the terms of use are set by the tool’s license (for example, whether commercial use is allowed), and the output can infringe someone else’s rights if it reproduces a recognizable style, trademark, or likeness.

items: Before commercial use, check the generator’s license — “free” does not mean “for everything”.
An image strikingly similar to a well-known brand or style is a legal risk regardless of the fact that “AI made it”.
Label AI content wherever your company, the platform, or the law requires it — and anywhere concealing it would undermine trust.

blocks.title: Rule of thumb
blocks.text: Not sure whether you can use something publicly? That is a question for the person responsible for legal/brand matters — BEFORE publication, not after.

screens.title: A human is responsible, not the model

blocks.text: AI is a tool — like a text editor or a spreadsheet. The offer, the report, or the reply to a customer goes out under your name, together with the errors the model made up and you let through. AI providers’ terms of service explicitly exclude their liability for the content of the outputs.

items: Verify the facts, figures, and names in AI content before you sign off on it.
Do not forward anything you cannot defend in your own words.
For important content, leave a verification trail: what was checked and in which source.

blocks.title: Remember
blocks.text: “That is what the AI generated” is not a line of defense — not with a customer, not with a manager, and not with the law.

interaction.title: Policy-compliant or shadow AI?
interaction.intro: The company SklepDemo allows customer data only in its internal AI assistant; public chats are permitted only for content with no company data. Assess the practices.

categories.label: Policy-compliant
categories.desc: An approved tool, or content with no company/customer data.

categories.label: Shadow AI / violation
categories.desc: An unapproved tool, data outside the company’s control, circumventing the rules.

items.text: An employee asks a public chat about general best practices for writing e-mails — with no company data at all.
items.rationale: General content, zero company data — a public chat is allowed.

items.text: A team pastes descriptions of customer tickets into private accounts in a public chat, “because it is faster”.
items.rationale: Customer data outside the approved tool — systemic shadow AI, regardless of the results.

items.text: An analyst works on customer data in the internal, approved AI assistant.
items.rationale: This is exactly what the internal tool was approved for.

items.text: A manager sends a confidential report to a private mailbox in order to run it through a favorite AI chat at home.
items.rationale: Confidential data leaves the company’s control twice over: private e-mail plus an unapproved tool.

items.text: An employee submits a new AI tool to the IT department for review and waits for the decision before using it.
items.rationale: The model path: innovation through review and approval, not on the quiet.

items.text: A developer pastes snippets of internal code into a public chat from a private account to find a bug faster.
items.rationale: Internal code is company data — a public chat from a private account breaks the policy.

items.text: Marketing uses an approved image generator and checks the commercial-use license before publication.
items.rationale: An approved tool plus license awareness before publication.

items.text: An intern sets up a “shared team account” in a public chat so the whole team can paste customer data there “officially”.
items.rationale: A shared account legalizes nothing — customer data still ends up in an unapproved tool.

points: Shadow AI = working on company data outside the company’s knowledge and control; good results do not offset a leak.
An AI policy is a map: approved tools times permitted data categories. Submit new tools for review.
You check the generator’s license before commercial use, and you are responsible for content that goes out under your name.
checklist: Is the tool I am using for this task approved for this type of data?
Can I defend the AI content in my own words before I sign off on it?
Do I know the path for submitting a new tool for review at my company?
summary.nextStep: Next step: the last module of the path — verifying AI content, that is, how to check the model’s answers before you pass them on.
